summary: I joined the International Longshore and Warehouse Union, or ILWU, in 1968 after working for Shell Chemical for ten years. My brothers were already involved in the union, one in Local 63 and the other in Local 13, and they helped me get started. I worked as a clerk and also did some longshore work early on. I remember participating in the 1971 ILWU strike and visiting the picket line during the 2002 lockout, though I had retired by then.

Before joining the ILWU, I worked for Shell Chemical for ten years. I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union during that time and served as secretary of our local group. I often clashed with the company over labor issues, which eventually led me to leave and pursue waterfront work. Handling heavy 500-pound drums at Shell was one of the toughest jobs I had.

entity: Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union
summary: While working at Shell Chemical, I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union, or OCAW. Our plant was 100 percent unionized, and I served as secretary of the union group for several years. I was involved in many disputes with the company, which was a challenging but important part of my early labor experience before moving to the waterfront.

I grew up in Grass Valley, California, where my father worked as a gold miner for 55 years. It was a small northern California town, and I have fond memories of the area. Later, I wrote poetry about the region, including about the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that ran nearby.

I served in the United States Army from 1954 to 1955. After my discharge, I moved to Oregon to work with my brother in logging. The Army experience was a brief but important part of my early adult life before I entered the labor force in logging and later on the waterfront.

After leaving the Army in 1955, I went to Oregon to work in the logging industry with my brother Ken. We worked out of Glendale, Oregon, where I set chokers behind his Caterpillar machinery. Unfortunately, the logging operation closed after about a year, which led me to move to Southern California to join the longshore unions.

I worked in Glendale, Oregon, for about a year after my Army service, helping my brother in logging operations. I set chokers while he operated heavy machinery. When the woods closed down there, I had to find work elsewhere, which eventually brought me to Southern California.

I moved to Southern California after logging in Oregon ended, where both my brothers were already involved in the longshore unions. They helped me get a job at Shell Chemical and later into the clerks union. I started longshore work there in 1968 and spent many years working at ports like Wilmington and San Pedro.

entity: International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union Local 13
summary: My brother Chuck was a member of ILWU Local 13, and he helped me get started on the waterfront in Southern California. Local 13 was one of the two locals my brothers belonged to, the other being Local 63. I worked alongside members of Local 13 during my early days on the docks and learned a lot from them.

entity: International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union Local 63
summary: My brother Raymond was in ILWU Local 63, the clerks union, and he helped me pass the test to get into the clerks hall in 1968. Local 63 was where I spent most of my career as a clerk, working at places like the Matson Company in Wilmington. Although I wasn’t active in union politics, Local 63 was an important part of my working life.

I grew up and worked in Northern California before moving down south. My father was a gold miner there for over five decades, and I worked in lumber companies in the region where I learned skills like forklift driving. Later, I wrote poetry about Northern California landmarks like the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that ran between Colfax and Nevada City.

My brother Chuck worked in Stockton, California, for three years after high school starting in 1949. He often talked about his experiences working on ships and about Harry Bridges, which gave me some early knowledge of the ILWU before I joined. Stockton was part of the longshore culture that influenced me.

I started my clerking job in 1968 at the Matson Company, located at Berth 198 in Wilmington, California. On my first day, I met a man named Morey Segal, who was ending his union career and moving into real estate. Working at Matson was a good introduction to clerking duties like checking cargo as it was loaded onto trucks.

Wilmington, Los Angeles, was where I worked as a longshore clerk starting in 1968, particularly at the Matson Company at Berth 198. It was also the location of the 1971 ILWU strike where I served full picket duty at Berth 146. Wilmington was a central place in my waterfront career and union experiences.

San Pedro was home to some of the men I worked with, including Morey Segal, who transitioned from the union to real estate there. I also owned an apartment building in San Pedro which I sold in 2002 before retiring. The community and waterfront in San Pedro were important parts of my life and career.

On my first day clerking at Matson in Wilmington, I met Morey Segal, who was a B-man in the union but was ending his career to go into real estate. Morey went on to do very well in real estate in San Pedro. He was one of the first people I encountered on the waterfront and left a positive impression.

I learned about the 1934 West Coast Waterfront Strike mostly through stories and reading. My brother Chuck talked about it, and I read accounts like the Rose Story. To me, it was a sad but glorious victory for the longshoremen, a strike that was meant to be won and was won, which shaped the union's future.

entity: 1971 International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union strike
summary: I was actively involved in the 1971 ILWU strike, serving full picket duty at Berth 146 in Wilmington. The picket line was a mix of hard work and camaraderie; we had a shack for food and a football game to pass the time. The bosses, including Local 94 leaders like Jim North, even brought us food trucks, and most of us managed financially during the strike thanks to savings and my wife's work.

Local 94 was involved during the 1971 ILWU strike, with leaders like Jim North playing a role. During the strike, the bosses associated with Local 94 brought food trucks to the picket line to support us. I remember Local 94 as part of the union leadership during that time, and their involvement was notable during the strike.

Jim North was one of the leaders in Local 94 during the 1971 ILWU strike. I recall that during the strike, he and others would come by the picket line with trucks of food for the strikers. His presence and support were part of the strike experience at Wilmington.

I retired in August 2002, just before the West Coast longshore lockout. I was recovering from a torn Achilles tendon and on crutches, so I couldn't join the picket line, but I did visit it briefly. Shortly after, I drove to Denver, Colorado, so my participation in the lockout was limited to support from a distance.

Right after the 2002 West Coast longshore lockout began, I drove to Denver, Colorado. I was recovering from an Achilles tendon injury and couldn't participate fully in the picket line, so Denver was where I spent that time away from the waterfront. It was a quiet period as I had just retired.

I wrote my first poem in the early 1970s about the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, which ran between Colfax and Nevada City, California. I used to see that little train when we drove to town, and it inspired me to capture the imagery of the small towns and the railroad in verse. It was a meaningful connection to my Northern California roots.

Colfax, California, was one of the towns connected by the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that I often saw as a child. It inspired me to write poetry about the area and the railroad in the early 1970s. Colfax represents part of my Northern California heritage and memories of the small-town life.

Nevada City, California, was another town along the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad route that I frequently saw growing up. It was part of the landscape that inspired my early poetry in the 1970s. Nevada City holds a special place in my memories of Northern California.

I heard about Harry Bridges from my brother Chuck, who worked on ships in Stockton after high school. He talked about Bridges and the union, which gave me some early insight into the ILWU before I joined. Harry Bridges was a legendary figure in the longshore union history that influenced many of us.
